background: An antique collector modeled the auction price $(y)$ of grandfather clocks as a function of the age of the clock $(x_1)$ and the number of bidders $(x_2)$. The hypothesized first-order model is $y=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2 x_2+\varepsilon$. The SPSS printout for the analysis is shown in Figure 12.6.

task.query: Find and interpret the adjusted coefficient of determination $R_{\mathrm{a}}^2$.
task.answer: The $R_{\mathrm{a}}^2$ value (highlighted in Figure 12.6) is .885. This implies that the least squares model has explained about $88.5 \%$ of the total sample variation in $y$ values (auction prices), after adjusting for sample size and number of independent variables in the model.

task.query: Conduct the global $F$-test of model usefulness at the $\alpha=.05$ level of significance.
task.answer:
The elements of the global test of the model follow:
$H_0: \beta_1=\beta_2=0$ (Note: $k=2$)
$H_a$: At least one of the two model coefficients, $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$, is nonzero.
Test statistic: $F=\frac{\text{MS(Model)}}{\text{MSE}}=\frac{2,141,531}{17,818}=120.19$ (see Figure 12.6)
$p$-value $\approx 0$
Conclusion: Because $\alpha=.05$ exceeds the observed significance level $(p \approx 0)$, the data provide strong evidence that at least one of the model coefficients is nonzero. The overall model appears to be statistically useful for predicting auction prices.

meta_info.caption: Assessing Overall Adequacy-Clock Auction Price Model
